  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. The source in `ExportBinderAsCPResource` is an HTML/XML `src` attribute from exported binder content, but `processMedia()` only accepts URLs beginning with `/m/` and resolves them through `mapperService.getMapperById(...)`, which looks up the session/anonymous mapper registry. `LogoMapper` is not registered there; it is registered only in the global mapper registry under `/g/logo/...`. Therefore the reported jump from `mapperService.getMapperById(...)` to `LogoMapper.handle(relPath, ...)` is not reachable at runtime. The tool effectively over-approximated the `Mapper.handle(...)` interface call and stitched together two different mapper subsystems. Also, for the real HTTP entry points, both `/m/` and `/g/` dispatchers reject subpaths containing `..` before invoking any mapper, so the classic traversal payload is already blocked.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "A2.Theoretically incorrect control flow"
  ],
  "evidence": "`ExportBinderAsCPResource.processMedia()` only handles mapped URLs and rejects anything not starting with `/m/`: `if(!src.startsWith(DispatcherModule.PATH_MAPPED)) return null;` then it extracts `id` and calls `Mapper mapper = mapperService.getMapperById(ureq.getUserSession(), id);` followed by `MediaResource resource = mapper.handle(mod, null);` ([src/main/java/org/olat/modules/portfolio/ui/export/ExportBinderAsCPResource.java:520-539]). `DispatcherModule` defines two distinct namespaces: `/m/` for normal mappers and `/g/` for global mappers ([src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/DispatcherModule.java:60-62]). `OpenOLATServlet` routes them to different dispatchers: `/m/ -> MapperDispatcher`, `/g/ -> GlobalMapperRegistry` ([src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/OpenOLATServlet.java:91-93]). `LogoMapper` is registered only as a global mapper: `logoUri = GlobalMapperRegistry.getInstance().register(\"logo\", new LogoMapper(this));` ([src/main/java/org/olat/admin/layout/LayoutModule.java:80]), and normal logo URLs are built as `/g/logo/optimized_<name>` ([src/main/java/org/olat/admin/layout/LayoutModule.java:126-127], [src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/GlobalMapperRegistry.java:94-96]). There is no registration of `LogoMapper` in `MapperService` (`rg` finds only the global registration). Finally, both actual dispatchers block traversal tokens before any mapper call: `if (mod.indexOf(\"..\") != -1) ... return/throw` in `MapperDispatcher` ([src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/MapperDispatcher.java:105-108]) and `GlobalMapperRegistry` ([src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/GlobalMapperRegistry.java:123-127]).",

  "analysis": "The reported source is real attacker-influenced content: `ExportBinderAsCPResource.ExportMedia.startElement()` reads `src`/`href` from parsed HTML/XML and passes them to `processMedia()`. The sink in `LogoMapper.handle()` (`new File(dir, relPath)`) could be security-sensitive if attacker-controlled input reached it. However, this specific path is not reachable in OpenOLAT. `processMedia()` only accepts URLs starting with `/m/` and resolves them through `mapperService.getMapperById(...)`, which is the session/cacheable mapper subsystem. `LogoMapper` is not registered there. It is registered only through `GlobalMapperRegistry.register(\"logo\", new LogoMapper(this))`, which exposes it under `/g/logo`, a different dispatcher path. Therefore a real logo URL (`/g/logo/optimized_<file>`) is rejected immediately by `processMedia()`, and a crafted `/m/logo/...` does not resolve to `LogoMapper` because `MapperService` has no such registration. That alone becomes exploitable because the resulting `File` objects are later used for real file operations in export/import code. This is not an unreachable or purely theoretical path: there is a concrete workflow where a crafted QTI assessment-test package is imported into the repository, then imported from the repository into the question pool, preserving malicious resource references. Later, exporting that pool item to the QTI editor or building an editor package will copy files resolved via those unsafe paths.   "evidence": "1) Reported unsafe path construction: `ImportExportHelper.java:72-75` does `String imgPath = img.getSrc().toString(); File imgFile = new File(directory, imgPath); if (imgFile.exists()) { ... }`. That method (`QTI21QPoolServiceProvider.java:201-224`) loads assessment items from the repository package and calls `importAssessmentItemRef()`. However, this is not a real CWE-022 in the observed logic. Before any `File` is used, `StaticServlet` calls `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)`. That helper does much more than re-encoding: it canonicalizes separators, prepends a leading slash, removes `//` and `/./`, and iteratively resolves `/../`; if traversal would escape the allowed root, it returns `null`, and `StaticServlet` immediately sends 404. On Linux/Unix, the local JDK `java.io.UnixFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` implementation also shows that `new File(parent, \"/x\")` is resolved as `parent + child`, not as filesystem-root `/x`, so an input like `/etc/passwd` stays under the servlet's static base. The only normalization gaps are trailing `/..` or `/foo/..`, but those resolve to directories under or at the static tree and are rejected by `staticFile.isDirectory()` with 403, not served. Therefore the reported path does not let an attacker read arbitrary files outside the intended static roots in practice. This is a false positive caused by missed sanitizer/guard semantics, not a truly exploitable traversal path.",

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The deletion sink is real and path-sensitive, but the reported SharePoint `DriveItem` name does not feasibly reach it in this workflow. IRIS stitched together a source leaf and a different target leaf across the copy/versioning logic. In the actual overwrite/version path, the external SharePoint item supplies only file content, while revision filenames and later deletion paths are derived from the existing local target file. Therefore this is not a real CWE-022 path traversal for the reported path.",
  "FP_Reason": ["A1"],
  "evidence": "Source: `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` returns `driveItem.name()` (DriveItemLeaf.java:87-89). However, when copying over an existing versioned file, the code calls `vfsRepositoryService.addVersion(currentLeaf, ..., newLeaf.getInputStream())` where `currentLeaf` is the existing local target and `newLeaf` is the source only for bytes (FolderController.java:2442-2446). Inside version creation, `addToRevisions()` uses `File currentFile = toFile(currentLeaf)` and then `generateFilenameForRevision(currentFile, ...)` (VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:1435, 1453-1455), so the stored `VFSRevision.filename` comes from the local target file name, not the SharePoint source name. The later sink deletes `new File(directory, fileToDelete)` using that stored revision filename (VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:1561-1587). For the non-version copy path, metadata copy also does not transfer filename/path taint: `copyTo(source, target, ...)` uses `toFile(target)` and `targetMetadata.copyValues(sourceMetadata, true)` (VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:969-980), while `copyValues()` copies descriptive fields only and does not copy `filename` or `relativePath` (VFSMetadataImpl.java:620-656; VFSTransientMetadata.java:532-568). So the reported taint path is a shallow, cross-object dataflow construction rather than a feasible runtime path.",

}"

Explaination: User-controlled request URI influences glossary path selection and reaches file creation/write via FileOutputStream(dst) without shown validation against absolute or '..' traversal, so this can enable writing/copying files at unintended locations.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive for CWE-022. The source is user-controlled request data, but the dangerous sink is not actually attacker-controlled in a traversal-capable way. Before the mapper is invoked, `MapperDispatcher.execute()` rejects any mapped path containing `..`, so the classic traversal payload the report relies on is blocked. After that, `GlossaryDefinitionMapper.handle()` does not use the whole request path as a filesystem path; it reconstructs a folder path as `canonicalRoot + repositoryHome + \"/\" + glossaryId + \"/_glossary_\"`, which keeps the user-influenced value confined to a single repository-id segment between a fixed root and a fixed suffix. The later write path in `GlossaryItemManager.getGlossaryFile()` is only to the constant file `glossary.xml` inside that folder, and only after `isFolderContainingGlossary()` has confirmed that the folder already contains `glossary.xml` or `glossary.textmarker.xml`; otherwise the request returns `NotFound` and no file is created. In real use, `glossaryId` is derived from `repoEntry.getOlatResource().getResourceableId()`, and repository resource roots are intentionally `/repository/<resourceId>`. So the tool over-approximated the path and missed the sanitizing / constraining logic; this is not a practical path traversal to arbitrary filesystem locations.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "1. Sanitizer missed: `MapperDispatcher.execute()` blocks traversal before dispatching the mapper: `String mod = slashPos > 0 ? subInfo.substring(slashPos) : \"\"; if (mod.indexOf(\"..\") != -1) { ... return; }` at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/dispatcher/mapper/MapperDispatcher.java:105-109`.\n2. Path is rooted, not arbitrary: `GlossaryDefinitionMapper.handle()` builds `glossaryFolderString = FolderConfig.getCanonicalRoot() + FolderConfig.getRepositoryHome() + \"/\" + glossaryId + \"/\" + GlossaryMarkupItemController.INTERNAL_FOLDER_NAME;` at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/control/generic/textmarker/GlossaryDefinitionMapper.java:63-65`. The attacker controls only `glossaryId`, not the filesystem root or written filename.\n3. Write only happens in an already-valid glossary folder: `GlossaryDefinitionMapper` first checks `if (!gIM.isFolderContainingGlossary(glossaryFolder)) return new NotFoundMediaResource();` at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/control/generic/textmarker/GlossaryDefinitionMapper.java:71-75`. Then `getGlossaryLastModifiedTime()` calls `getGlossaryFile()`, which writes only constant `glossary.xml` via `folderContainingGlossary.createChildLeaf(GLOSSARY_FILENAME)` at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/modules/glossary/GlossaryItemManager.java:201-225`.\n4. Real application context constrains `glossaryId` to repository resource IDs: `CourseGlossaryFactory` sets `String glossaryId = repoEntry.getOlatResource().getResourceableId().toString();` at `src/main/java/org/olat/course/run/glossary/CourseGlossaryFactory.java:77-79`, and `FileResourceManager.getFileResourceRootImpl()` defines the resource root as `/repository/<resourceableId>` at `src/main/java/org/olat/fileresource/FileResourceManager.java:94-95`.",

The shown sanitization is not demonstrated to block absolute or '..' traversal safely, so this path can be vulnerable to path traversal.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The source is valid: `getDestinationPath()` reads the user-controlled WebDAV `Destination` header and decodes it. The sink is also valid: the resulting path is resolved by `VFSResourceRoot.resolveFile()` into `VFSManager.resolveFile()`, which for local folders does `new File(base, childName)` and accepts `childName == \"..\"` if that directory exists. The key sanitizer is insufficient: `RequestUtil.normalize()` explicitly says it is not a security check, and it only removes `\"/../\"` patterns, not a terminal `\"/..\"`. Therefore a destination ending in `.../public/..` survives normalization and resolves to the filesystem parent of the mounted folder. This is not just a theoretical taint flow: in `COPY`/`MOVE`, once the destination resolves to that escaped parent directory, the servlet recursively builds `childDest = dest + \"/\" + entry.getName()` for collection copies without renormalizing it, and `VFSResourceRoot.write()` then creates files under that escaped parent. There is no later canonical-root containment check during `resolve()`. The only containment check (`LocalFolderImpl.isInPath`) runs when creating a child under the already-escaped parent, so it no longer protects the original WebDAV root. This can cause unauthorized filesystem access outside the intended mounted WebDAV subtree.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant code path: `WebDAVDispatcherImpl.getDestinationPath()` decodes the header and calls `RequestUtil.normalize()` ([WebDAVDispatcherImpl.java:1639-1690], [RequestUtil.java:95-147]). `RequestUtil.normalize()` itself warns it is not for malicious input and only collapses `\"/../\"`; a trailing `\"/..\"` is left unchanged. The resolved path then reaches `VFSResourceRoot.resolveFile()` -> `base.resolve(name)` -> `LocalFolderImpl.resolve()` -> `VFSManager.resolveFile()` ([VFSResourceRoot.java:336-339], [LocalFolderImpl.java:469-476], [VFSManager.java:170-195]). In `VFSManager.resolveFile()`, local resolution does `File t = new File(l.getBasefile().getAbsolutePath(), childName);` and if `childName` is `\"..\"`, Java resolves that to the parent directory. Exploitability in surrounding logic: `copyResource()` accepts the header-derived destination and for directory copies recursively computes `childDest = dest + \"/\" + entry.getName()` ([WebDAVDispatcherImpl.java:1545-1615], [WebDAVDispatcherImpl.java:1707-1756]). `VFSResourceRoot.write()` then resolves `parentPath` and calls `folder.createChildLeaf(name)` on that resolved container ([VFSResourceRoot.java:173-201]). `LocalFolderImpl.createChildLeaf()` only checks `isInPath(name)` relative to that current folder, not relative to the original WebDAV mount ([LocalFolderImpl.java:530-547]). Realistic scenario: an authenticated WebDAV user with write access to a mounted folder such as `home/public` can copy or move a collection to `Destination: .../webdav/home/public/..`; the destination becomes the parent directory on disk, and the recursive child writes land outside the intended `public` mount. The same resolver weakness is broader than the report: normal request paths are not normalized at all in `getRelativePath()` ([WebDAVDispatcherImpl.java:399-406]), so paths like `/webdav/home/public/../private/...` can also resolve across sibling folders.",

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source and sink are real, but the reported CWE-022 is not exploitable in this code path. `request.getPathInfo()` is user-controlled and reaches `new File(...)`, so B1 does not apply. The problem is the tool misinterprets the code semantics: `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` is not a weak/no-op sanitizer, because it canonicalizes `//`, `/./`, resolves `/../`, and returns `null` when traversal would escape the logical root. `StaticServlet` checks that `null` and rejects the request. In addition, the report appears to misunderstand Java `File(File,String)` behavior in this codebase: a child path beginning with `/` does not escape the configured parent root here, so the constructed `File` stays under the intended static/customizing directory. This is therefore not an A1/A2 reachability issue, and not a C1 prompt issue; it is primarily a code-semantics error (D2), with a missed sanitizer as additional context.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D2"],
  "evidence": "1) Public entry point: `src/main/webapp-tomcat/WEB-INF/web.xml:135-138` maps `rawservlet` to `/raw/*`, so `StaticServlet.doGet()` is reachable from external requests.\n2) Reported path construction: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:100-108` takes `/raw/static/...`, extracts `staticRelPath`, normalizes it, and builds `File file = new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath);`.\n3) Actual sanitizer: `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/media/ServletUtil.java:667-692` replaces backslashes, adds a leading `/`, collapses `//`, removes `/./`, and resolves `/../`. Crucially, at lines 687-690 it does `if (index == 0) return (null); // Trying to go outside our context`. Therefore inputs like `../../etc/passwd` normalize to `null`, and `StaticServlet` rejects them at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:102-105`.\n4) Java/File semantics invalidate the claimed absolute-path escape. The project itself relies on this exact constructor pattern in `src/main/java/org/olat/admin/sysinfo/manager/CustomStaticFolderManager.java:49-69`, where `STATIC_FOLDER = \"/customizing/static/\"` and `getRootFile()` returns `new File(WebappHelper.getUserDataRoot(), STATIC_FOLDER)`. If a leading `/` in the child escaped the parent, this manager would point to filesystem root `/customizing/static/`, which is not the intended design.\n5) Runtime confirmation in this environment: a JShell check printed `new File(new File(\"/base/root\"), \"/etc/passwd\").getPath()` as `/base/root/etc/passwd`, not `/etc/passwd`. So even an input that normalizes to `/etc/passwd` remains under the configured root directory rather than escaping it.\n6) Additional safeguard: if an odd path such as `/raw/static/a/..` survives normalization as a directory reference, `StaticServlet` blocks directories with `file.isDirectory()` and sends forbidden at `src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:109-114`.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

Explaination: Jar entries come from a ZIP/JAR and may contain attacker-controlled '../' or absolute paths. That name is used directly to build targetFile and write it without a containment check, so this is a Zip Slip/path traversal risk.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The source and sink are correctly identified: `JarEntry.getName()` comes from the uploaded JAR and is attacker-controlled, and `copyLanguagesFromJar()` uses that name directly in `new File(targetBaseDir, jarEntryName)` and then writes it with `FileUtils.save(...)` without any canonicalization or containment check. This is a real Zip Slip style path traversal/arbitrary file write. The path is also concretely reachable: the only caller is `I18nConfigSubImportLangController`, which uploads a JAR, scans it with `sarchForAvailableLanguagesInJarFile(tmpJar, true)`, and then calls `i18nManager.copyLanguagesFromJar(tmpJar, importLangKeys)`. The pre-scan is not a sanitizer; it only checks whether an entry contains `org/olat/core/_i18n` and `LocalStrings_`, so a crafted entry such as `../../../../org/olat/core/_i18n/LocalStrings_en.properties` can both pass the scan and later be written outside the intended base directory. This is not an A1/A2 false path, because the control flow is direct and reachable. It is also not B1/D1/C1: the sink is genuine, the source is genuine, and no effective path validation exists. Exploitability is limited because the feature only runs in translation-server mode (`is.translation.server=enabled`, disabled by default) and through an admin/system-admin translation workflow, but if such a privileged user imports a malicious language pack, the traversal can write outside the configured source directories.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reachable call chain: `I18nConfigSubImportLangController.formInnerEvent()` uploads a JAR and calls `sarchForAvailableLanguagesInJarFile(tmpJar, true)`; `formOK()` then calls `i18nManager.copyLanguagesFromJar(tmpJar, importLangKeys)`. Vulnerable sink in `I18nManager.copyLanguagesFromJar()` at lines around 1937-1951: `String jarEntryName = jarEntry.getName(); ... if (jarEntryName.endsWith(I18N_DIRNAME + \"/\" + LocalStrings_<lang> + \".properties\")) { File targetFile = new File(targetBaseDir, jarEntryName); targetFile.getParentFile().mkdirs(); FileUtils.save(jar.getInputStream(jarEntry), targetFile); }`. No normalization/check of `targetFile.getCanonicalPath()` against `targetBaseDir`. `FileUtils.save(InputStream, File)` is only a raw stream-to-file copy and does not sanitize paths. Pre-scan weakness in `sarchForAvailableLanguagesInJarFile()` around 1898-1910: it accepts entries if `jarEntryName.indexOf(\"org/olat/core/_i18n\") != -1` and contains `LocalStrings_`; it does not reject `../` or absolute paths. Deployment context: `copyLanguagesFromJar()` is gated by `i18nModule.isTransToolEnabled()`, and `serviceconfig/olat.properties` sets `is.translation.server=disabled` by default; the translation UI is registered under `SystemAdminMainController`, so exploitation requires a privileged admin-style workflow, but the traversal itself is still real when that mode is enabled.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

Explaination: User-controlled request URI flows into file resolution under the application root and is later opened with FileInputStream. This is a plausible path traversal if relPath is not strictly constrained against absolute or '..' segments.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported source (`request.getRequestURI()`) is user-controlled, and the downstream sink (`FileInputStream` through `LocalFileImpl`) is real, so this is not a bogus source/sink pairing by itself. However, the claimed CWE-022 exploit path is not feasible in the real code path. Before `LibraryThumbnailMapper.handle()` is invoked, `MapperDispatcher.execute()` validates the mapped suffix and rejects any request containing `..` with HTTP 403. Since `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` decodes the URI before that check, encoded `..` payloads are also covered. As a result, the dangerous traversal values needed to escape the intended directory never reach `VFSManager.olatRootLeaf()` or the later file open. In practice, a user probing `../` traversal here would be blocked, not reach arbitrary file disclosure or crash behavior.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Key sanitizer on the reported path: `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` decodes the URI (`requestUri = URLDecoder.decode(requestUri, UTF8_ENCODING);`, DispatcherModule.java:126-130). Then `MapperDispatcher.execute()` checks the exact path segment passed to the mapper: `String mod = slashPos > 0 ? subInfo.substring(slashPos) : \"\"; if (mod.indexOf(\"..\") != -1) { ... hres.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_FORBIDDEN); return; }` (MapperDispatcher.java:105-109). Only after that does it call `m.handle(mod, hreq)` (line 112). The library mapper sink is downstream of this guard: `if (relPath.startsWith(basePath)) { VFSLeaf file = VFSManager.olatRootLeaf(relPath); ... }` (LibraryThumbnailMapper.java:54-57). The file resolution itself is rooted under the VFS storage root, not taken as a raw absolute path: `new File(FolderConfig.getCanonicalRoot() + fileRelPath)` (VFSManager.java:65-67). Therefore the report missed a decisive sanitizer and treated an infeasible traversal input as if it could reach the sink.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The reachable vulnerability is a Zip Slip style path traversal during page/course import. A user-supplied course archive reaches `PageCourseNode.importNode()`, which opens nested `page.zip`; `PageImportExportHelper.importPage()` then reads attacker-controlled `page.xml` and passes the same ZIP to `PageServiceImpl.importPage()`. In `PageServiceImpl.unzip()`, the code derives `fileName` from `ZipEntry.getName()` using `entryPath.replace(mediaZipPath, \"\")`, then builds `new File(mediaDir, fileName)` and writes it with `FileUtils.copyToFile(...)` without any canonical-path check, normalization, or rejection of `..` / absolute paths. The only checks are `mediaFile.isHidden()` and `mediaFile.getName().startsWith(\".\")`, which do not stop `../../.../target` traversal. This means a malicious archive can escape `mediaDir` and overwrite files writable by the OpenOLAT process, causing integrity compromise and potentially DoS or further impact depending on filesystem permissions. One nuance: the IRIS tail through persisted `rootFilename` to later `FileInputStream` is not the key exploit step, because `mediaFile.getName()` stores only the basename; however the earlier write sink in `unzip()` is real and sufficient to confirm CWE-22.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reachable import path: `CourseHandler.importResource()` -> `CourseFactory.importCourseFromZip()` -> node import -> `PageCourseNode.importNode()` (`src/main/java/org/olat/course/nodes/PageCourseNode.java:228-235`) opens `page.zip` and calls `PageImportExportHelper.importPage()`. `PageImportExportHelper.importPage()` (`src/main/java/org/olat/modules/ceditor/manager/PageImportExportHelper.java:191-195`) reads `page.xml` and calls `pageService.importPage(pageOwner, mediaOwner, page, zfile)`. Vulnerable logic: `PageServiceImpl.importMediaVersion()` iterates ZIP entries and calls `unzip(...)` when `entryPath.startsWith(mediaZipPath)` (`src/main/java/org/olat/modules/ceditor/manager/PageServiceImpl.java:414-418`); `unzip()` does `String fileName = entry.getName().replace(mediaZipPath, \"\"); File mediaFile = new File(mediaDir, fileName); FileUtils.copyToFile(in, mediaFile, \"\");` (`PageServiceImpl.java:449-458`). Sink behavior: `FileUtils.copyToFile()` creates parent directories and opens `new FileOutputStream(targetFile)` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/FileUtils.java:464-472`), so the computed path is actually written. Practical exploit example: an attacker crafts `page.xml` so `mediaVersion.storagePath` matches a chosen prefix, then includes a ZIP entry like `<prefix>../../../../some/writable/path/poc.txt`; after `replace(...)`, the child path contains traversal and is written outside `mediaDir`.",

However, the reported CWE-022 is not exploitable in the actual code path. Before any file access, every attacker-controlled suffix is passed through `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)`, which replaces backslashes, ensures a leading slash, removes `//` and `/./`, resolves `/../` segments, and returns `null` if traversal would escape above the root. `StaticServlet` immediately returns 404 when normalization returns `null`. Residual edge cases such as `/..` or `/dir/..` do not yield arbitrary file reads: they only resolve to directories, and the servlet explicitly rejects directories with 403 before opening any file. The report's claim about 'absolute paths' is also overstated for the documented Tomcat/Unix deployment, because Java `File(parent, child)` does not let a child like `/etc/passwd` override the parent here; it is resolved under the static base. So this is a false positive caused primarily by missing the sanitizer and the post-normalization directory guard.",

The sanitizer in `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` (lines 667-691) performs `normalized = normalized.replace('\\\\', '/')`, prepends `/` if needed, collapses `//` and `/./`, and repeatedly resolves `/../`; if `/../` appears at the beginning, it returns `null` (`if (index == 0) return null;`). 3. File construction happens only after normalization: `new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath)` at line 108 and `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)` at line 156. 4. Even the remaining suffix cases are blocked: `staticFile.isDirectory()` leads to `sendForbidden(response)` at lines 192-196, and the customizing branch does the same at lines 109-114. D1: there is no effective sanitizer. The code never canonicalizes or normalizes the destination path and never checks that the resolved file stays under `WebappHelper.getTmpDir()`. The MIME check is extension-based only, and the size check is performed on `image.length()` before the copy, so it does not prevent traversal. One detail in the report explanation is overstated: a leading absolute path is not the main issue here because the code prepends the tmp dir as a string. However, `../` traversal is still enough to escape the temp directory, so the core CWE-022 finding remains valid. Then `createTaxonomyLevelToImageMap()` does `fileName = zipEntry.getName().replaceAll(\".+background/\", \"\")`, yielding `../../../../var/tmp/poc.png`; next it builds `image = new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + \"/\" + fileName)` and writes with `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)`. Because there is no `getCanonicalPath()`/`normalize()` + prefix check, the filesystem resolves `..` segments and the write escapes the tmp directory. The later code in `TaxonomyTreeTableController` uses the resulting `File` object directly (`storeBackgroundImage(..., backImage, backImage.getName())`), confirming the path is treated as legitimate. Before `LibraryThumbnailMapper.handle()` is called, the dispatcher rejects any `mod` containing `..` and returns HTTP 403. `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` also URL-decodes the URI first, so normal encoded traversal sequences are checked in decoded form. 46:
{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report’s source is real: `request.getPathInfo()` is user-controlled and reaches `new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath)` in `StaticServlet`. However, the sink is misinterpreted for CWE-22. OpenOLAT first calls `ServletUtil.normalizePath()`, which canonicalizes separators, prepends `/`, collapses `//` and `/./`, and most importantly rejects traversal attempts that would escape above the logical root by returning `null` when `/../` occurs at the beginning after normalization. For inputs like `../../etc/passwd`, normalization becomes `/../../etc/passwd`, hits `index == 0`, and returns `null`, so the request is rejected. For inputs like `a/../../etc/passwd`, repeated reduction also reaches a leading `/../` and returns `null`. The tool’s explanation also assumes that the leading slash in the normalized path makes `new File(parent, child)` escape the parent directory. That is incorrect in this runtime context: Java’s `File(File parent, String child)` resolves the child against the parent in a system-dependent way, and on Unix `UnixFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` concatenates `parent + child` when `child` starts with `/` and `parent` is not `/`. So `new File(root, \"/foo\")` becomes `<root>/foo`, not `/foo`. Since this project runs with a Unix JDK in the analyzed environment, the reported path does not permit traversal outside the intended static roots. The vulnerability is therefore a false positive caused by misunderstanding the sink semantics rather than a real exploitable CWE-22.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. B1: the source and sink are correctly identified. The source is the SCORM manifest item's `identifier` attribute, read directly with `element.getAttributeValue(CP_Core.IDENTIFIER)`. That value is later used as the `itemId`/`scoID` and reaches `SettingsHandler.getScoDataModelFile(String itemId)`, which does `new File(getFilePath(), itemId + \".xml\")` with no normalization, canonical-path check, or character allowlist. This is a real filesystem path construction sink for CWE-22. A1/A2: the path is concretely reachable, not just a shallow or impossible flow. OpenOLAT accepts imported SCORM packages through `SCORMCPHandler.acceptImport()` and `ScormCPFileResource.validateImsManifest()`, but that validator only checks package structure, resource references, and identifier uniqueness; it does not schema-validate the manifest or reject `/`, `..`, or absolute-path style identifiers. At runtime, the malicious identifier is used during session initialization and SCO launch: `ScormSessionController.init()` -> `ScormManager.init()` / `createNavLinks()` -> `SequenceManager.addNewItem()` -> `ItemSequence.setItemId()` -> later `ScormSessionController.launchItem()` -> `LMSDataHandler.getCMIData()` -> `findItemFromIndex()` -> `ItemSequence.loadInModel()` -> `ScoDocument.loadDocument()` -> `settings.getScoDataModelFile(scoID)` -> `exists()` / `super.loadDocument(pathToNavFile)`. D1: there is no sanitizer on this path. In fact, the code has an even stronger issue than the report states: during `ScormPackageHandler.buildSettings()`, the same untrusted identifier is used to create and save the SCO XML file, so a crafted SCORM package can traverse out of the intended SCORM storage directory and write an XML file elsewhere under the server's filesystem permissions. Real-world impact requires the attacker to control a SCORM package that is imported into OpenOLAT (or convince a privileged user to import it), but that is a realistic threat model for course-content platforms. The forced `.xml` suffix limits arbitrary-file targeting somewhat, yet this is still a real path traversal with out-of-directory file access and potential overwrite/crash impact.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant code points: `ScormManager.createNavLinks()` reads `String itemId = element.getAttributeValue(CP_Core.IDENTIFIER);` and stores it via `_sequence.addNewItem(itemId, ...)`. `SequenceManager.addNewItem()` calls `anItem.setItemId(scoId);` and stores the item in `_itemSequences`. Later, `LMSDataHandler.getCMIData()` resolves the runtime numeric index back to the manifest identifier with `findItemFromIndex(Integer.parseInt(itemIndex))`, and `loadinModel(itemId)` calls `theCourse.getSequence().getItem(itemId).loadInModel()`. In `ItemSequence.loadInModel()`, `anSco.loadDocument(_scoitemID);` is invoked. In `ScoDocument.loadDocument(String scoID)`, the sink is `File pathToNavFile = settings.getScoDataModelFile(scoID); if (pathToNavFile.exists()) { super.loadDocument(pathToNavFile); }`. The path builder is `SettingsHandler.getScoDataModelFile(String itemId) { return new File(getFilePath(), itemId + \".xml\"); }`. There is no check for `..`, `/`, `\\\\`, or canonical containment. Import acceptance is weak: `SCORMCPHandler.acceptImport()` delegates to `ScormCPFileResource.evaluate()`, and `ScormCPFileResource.validateImsManifest()` only checks structure and uniqueness (`if (!set.add(identifier)) return false;`) but does not validate identifier syntax against SCORM/IMS schema. Also, `XMLUtils.readXMLFile()` explicitly reads XML \"without Schema Validation\". Stronger still, `ScormPackageHandler.iterateThruManifest()` uses the same manifest `id` to create files: `File scoFile = settings.getScoDataModelFile(id); ... scoModel.setFile(scoFile); ... scoModel.saveDocument();`, and `XMLUtils.write2XMLFile()` does `parent.mkdirs(); new FileOutputStream(file);`, enabling out-of-directory XML file creation/overwrite if `id` contains traversal segments. If the traversed-to XML is missing or malformed, `ScoDocument.loadDocument()` throws `OLATRuntimeException`, so a malicious package can at minimum break SCORM playback; if the target path is writable, it can overwrite/create XML files outside the intended SCORM directory.",

  "analysis": "The reported source (`HttpServletRequest.getPathInfo()`) and sink (`new File(...)` followed by file serving) are on a real reachable path, so this is not an A1/A2 issue. It is also not a B1 issue, because those program points could be dangerous in general. The false positive comes from a missed sanitizer (D1): `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` normalizes separators, resolves `/./` and `/../`, and returns `null` when traversal would escape above the allowed root. As a result, inputs such as `../../etc/passwd` or `a/../../etc/passwd` are rejected before the `File` object is used. The remaining edge case of a trailing `/..` is not a file-read bypass here, because it resolves to a directory path and the servlet explicitly blocks directories with `sendForbidden(response)`. Therefore the reported path does not produce a real CWE-022 arbitrary file read in this code path.",

The sanitizer is in `src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/media/ServletUtil.java`, lines 653-695: it replaces `\\\\` with `/`, prepends `/` if needed, removes `//`, removes `/./`, and for `/../` it executes `if (index == 0) return null;` before collapsing the segment. So `/raw/static/../../etc/passwd` becomes `null` and is rejected; `/raw/static/a/../../etc/passwd` is reduced until it also hits `index == 0` and is rejected. The report’s concern about leading `/` is also not enough here: Java `File(parent, child)` does not discard the parent in this situation; the JDK `File` constructor resolves the child against the parent, and on Unix a child starting with `/` is concatenated as `parent + child`, so `/etc/passwd` would map to `<root>/etc/passwd`, not the filesystem root. This confirms the path does not escape the intended base directory via the reported flow.",

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The sink in `VFSManager.resolveFile()` is real, and `sanitizePath()` by itself is not a traversal defense. However, this specific reported request-to-file path is not exploitable as CWE-022 in OpenOLAT. The mapped-request dispatcher blocks traversal payloads before `LLRunController.LLMapper.handle()` is reached, and `LLMapper` only resolves paths that exactly match preconfigured internal-link targets. So the report connects a real source and a real sink, but misses the decisive runtime guard and allowlist that make traversal infeasible in this flow.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1.Missed Key Program Points"
  ],
  "evidence": "`MapperDispatcher.java:105-109` rejects any mapped suffix containing `..` before calling the mapper: `String mod = slashPos > 0 ? subInfo.substring(slashPos) : \"\"; if (mod.indexOf(\"..\") != -1) { ... hres.setStatus(HttpServletResponse.SC_FORBIDDEN); return; }`. This check happens before `m.handle(mod, hreq)` at line 112. `DispatcherModule.subtractContextPath()` also URL-decodes the request URI when needed, so encoded traversal like `%2e%2e` is not a bypass. The subtle point in the IRIS explanation is its assumption that a normalized path beginning with `/` causes `new File(base, normalizedRelPath)` to ignore `base` and become absolute. That is false on the Linux/Unix JDK behavior relevant here. JDK `File(String parent, String child)` delegates to `UnixFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)`, and if `child` starts with `/`, it returns `parent + child` (trimmed), not an escaped absolute path. So `new File(staticAbsPath, \"/images/x.png\")` resolves under `staticAbsPath`, and `new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), \"/foo\")` resolves under the customizing root. The fallback path in `deliverStatic()` is also anchored because it uses `WebappHelper.getContextRealPath(STATIC_DIR_NAME + fallbackPath)` and `getRealPath(STATIC_DIR_NAME + normalizedRelPath)`, both still prefixed with `/static`.   "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source and sink are real and the control flow is reachable: `HttpServletRequest.getPathInfo()` feeds `StaticServlet`, which eventually constructs `File` objects and serves them. However, this is still a false positive because the request path is sanitized before every relevant sink, and the remaining path is confined to the intended static roots. `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` replaces backslashes, forces a leading slash, collapses `//` and `/./`, and repeatedly resolves `/../`; if traversal would escape the logical root, it returns `null`, after which `StaticServlet` sends 404 instead of accessing the filesystem. The report’s claim that the normalization does not protect against `..` is incorrect for the actual code path. The residual `.../..` case only resolves to a directory, and `StaticServlet` explicitly blocks directories with 403. This FP is best explained by D1 (missed sanitizer / misread sanitizer), not by unreachable control flow or wrong source selection.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],
  "evidence": "1. All user-controlled branches normalize before file access: `StaticServlet.doGet()` calls `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)` for the `_noversion_`, `/static`, versioned, and fallback paths before constructing `File` objects.\n2. The sanitizer is substantive, not cosmetic. In `ServletUtil.normalizePath()`: it converts `\\\\` to `/`, prepends `/` if missing, removes `//`, removes `/./`, and resolves `/../` in a loop; if `/../` occurs at index 0 it returns `null` (`if (index == 0) return null;`). So inputs such as `../../etc/passwd`, `..\\\\..\\\\secret`, or nested internal traversals are rejected or collapsed back inside the static tree.\n3. The remaining edge case `path/..` does not produce file disclosure. It becomes a directory path under the static root, and `StaticServlet` later checks `staticFile.isDirectory()` and sends 403.\n4. The report’s absolute-path concern is also unsupported by actual Java semantics. Official JDK path resolution for `new File(parent, child)` appends a slash-prefixed child under the parent here rather than discarding the parent: on Unix, `UnixFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` returns `parent + child` when `child` starts with `/`; on Windows, `WinNTFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` drops the leading slash and still appends under `parent`. Therefore a normalized path like `/images/logo.png` resolves to `<static-root>/images/logo.png`, not to a filesystem absolute path such as `/images/logo.png` or `C:\\images\\logo.png`.\n5. The served roots are fixed application/static roots: normal static delivery uses the webapp `/static` directory, and the `/raw/static/...` customizing branch uses `CustomStaticFolderManager.getRootFile()` under `WebappHelper.getUserDataRoot() + \"/customizing/static/\"`. The request path cannot escape those roots through the reported flow.\n6. Real-world impact: this path does not let an attacker read arbitrary files outside the static/customizing roots, nor does it create a crash condition beyond normal 404/403 handling.",

}"

Explaination: User-controlled request path reaches File construction/existence check under a root directory with only a weak normalization step; no evidence of blocking absolute paths or '..' traversal, so this can enable path traversal.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "Type B1: the source (`request.getPathInfo()`) and sink (`new File(...)` followed by existence/read checks) are real, but the path is not actually exploitable for CWE-022 in this code. Type A1/A2: the reported path is reachable, so this is not mainly a shallow-callgraph or unreachable-control-flow issue. The false positive comes from code semantics: `StaticServlet` normalizes every user-controlled path before file use, and `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` removes `//`, `/./`, converts `\\\\` to `/`, and returns `null` when traversal would escape above the logical root via `/../`. In addition, the report's 'absolute path' concern is incorrect for Java: `new File(parent, child)` does not let a child like `/etc/passwd` escape the parent here; on Unix it resolves under `parent`, and Windows uses similar parent-relative resolution for this form. Real-world result: requests such as `/raw/static/../../etc/passwd` are rejected, and payloads that normalize to directories like `/..` only hit a directory check and return 403. This does not provide arbitrary file read outside OpenOLAT's static/customizing roots, so the report is a false positive.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "Relevant flow in `StaticServlet`: all three user-controlled branches call `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)` before any file access (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/servlets/StaticServlet.java:91-93`, `101-103`, `125-127`). The normalizer (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/gui/media/ServletUtil.java:653-695`) does the following: replaces backslashes with slashes (`668`), prepends `/` if needed (`669`), collapses `//` (`671-676`), removes `/./` (`678-683`), and, crucially, resolves `/../` while returning `null` if traversal would go above the root (`685-691`). Therefore a classic payload like `../../etc/passwd` becomes `/../../etc/passwd`, immediately hits `index == 0`, and returns `null`, after which `StaticServlet` sends 404 (`StaticServlet.java:93-95`, `103-105`, `127-129`).\n\nThe reported sink in the customizing branch is `new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath)` (`StaticServlet.java:107-108`), where the root is fixed to `userdata/customizing/static/` (`src/main/java/org/olat/admin/sysinfo/manager/CustomStaticFolderManager.java:49`, `68-69`). The normal static branch uses `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)` with `staticAbsPath` anchored to `/static` inside the webapp (`StaticServlet.java:144-156`). The fallback path is also still rooted under `/static` because it calls `WebappHelper.getContextRealPath(STATIC_DIR_NAME + fallbackPath)` (`StaticServlet.java:171-179`), and `getContextRealPath` simply resolves that servlet-context path (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/WebappHelper.java:224-229`).\n\nThe report's absolute-path theory is also incorrect. JDK 21 `java.io.File` delegates to `FileSystem.resolve(parent, child)`. In the local Unix JDK source, `resolve(String parent, String child)` returns `parent + child` when `child` starts with `/` and `parent` is not `/`; it does not discard the parent. So a normalized child `/etc/passwd` becomes `<staticRoot>/etc/passwd`, not the OS file `/etc/passwd`. Windows resolution is likewise parent-relative for this form. Finally, even edge cases such as `..` or `dir/..` only resolve to directories under or adjacent to the static root, and the servlet explicitly blocks directories with `sendForbidden(...)` (`StaticServlet.java:109-114`, `182-190`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

So a crafted QTI package with a nested item path can legally use package-internal references such as `../../shared.png`, have that exact relative path preserved in storage, and then cause traversal when the item is exported into a shallower destination. This is reachable through normal product flows: pool file import (`ImportController -> qpoolService.importItems -> QTI21ImportProcessor.process(File)`) and later editor/test export (`AssessmentTestComposerController.doInsert -> exportToQTIEditor`, `QTI21AssessmentTestHandler.createResource -> exportToEditorPackage`).", "FP_Reason": [], "evidence": "`ImportExportHelper.java:71-75` stores the original path string: `String imgPath = img.getSrc().toString(); File imgFile = new File(directory, imgPath); ... materials.addMaterial(new ItemMaterial(imgFile, imgPath));`. `QTI21ExportProcessor.java:148-153` and `251-256` later do: `String exportPath = material.getExportUri(); File exportFile = new File(questionContainer/containerDir, exportPath); if(!exportFile.getParentFile().exists()) { exportFile.getParentFile().mkdirs(); } FileUtils.bcopy(originalFile, exportFile, ...)`. There is no export-side sanitizer; `AssessmentItemChecker` only fixes response/template declaration types. Import only enforces package-root containment: `QTI21ImportProcessor.java:218-226` resolves `assessmentItemPath.getParent().resolve(material)`, checks `normalizedMaterialPath.startsWith(parentPath)`, then copies with `PathUtils.copyFileToDir(materialFile, outputFile.getParentFile(), material)`, which preserves the original `material` string including `..`. Explaination: User-controlled HREF from XML attribute is used to build a File under rootFolder without checks against absolute paths or '..' traversal before filesystem access.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source is real: a SCORM author controls the `href` attribute in `imsmanifest.xml`, and `CP_Core.getElementHREF()` returns that value with only slash normalization and optional `xml:base` prefixing. However, the reported sink is not a practical CWE-22 sink in this project. `CP_Core.getAbsoluteURL()` only constructs `new File(getRootFolder(), href)` and calls `exists()` to decide whether to return a `file:///...` string or treat the value as an external URL. Along the reported path, OpenOLAT does not actually open, stream, or persist the traversed file. In `ScormPackageHandler.iterateThruManifest()`, the result is only stored in a local variable and checked with `startsWith(\"file:///\")`; in the runtime player, the SCORM content is not loaded from this `launchUrl` path. The actual delivery path uses the raw manifest `href` through a different code path. So this report is a false positive for the specific `CP_Core.getAbsoluteURL()` / `new File(...)` path that IRIS reported.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Reported path: `CP_Core.getElementHREF()` reads `element.getAttributeValue(HREF)` (CP_Core.java:475), then `getAbsoluteURL()` does `String tmp = href.replaceAll(\"%20\", \" \"); File file = new File(getRootFolder(), tmp); if(file.exists()) { return new FilePath(file, params).getURL(); }` (CP_Core.java:423-435). But this is not the real exploit sink. First, `ScormPackageHandler.iterateThruManifest()` assigns `url = getAbsoluteURL(element)` and only uses it for a string branch before discarding it (ScormPackageHandler.java:164-181). Second, `NavigationViewer.getLaunch()` calls `_scormCore.getAbsoluteURL(element)` (NavigationViewer.java:234), and `ScormManager` stores that as `launchUrl` (ScormManager.java:313-317), but `SequenceManager` merely stores/returns it and uses it only for status filtering; it is not the path that serves SCORM content (SequenceManager.java:111-116, 236-256). Third, the real runtime loading path is elsewhere: `ScormCPManifestTreeModel` stores the raw manifest `href` as the tree node user object (ScormCPManifestTreeModel.java:90-97, 171-180), and `ScormAPIandDisplayController.setSco()` loads the iframe with `iframeCtr.setCurrentURI(identifierRes)` (ScormAPIandDisplayController.java:503-512). Therefore the IRIS report picked the wrong sink/path for the claimed CWE-22.",
  "new_reason:": "There is a separate path worth auditing: the raw SCORM `href` is passed into the iframe/VFS delivery stack (`setCurrentURI` -> `IFrameDeliveryMapper` -> `rootDir.resolve(path)`), and `VFSManager.sanitizePath()` does not remove `..`. That is outside the reported `CP_Core.getAbsoluteURL()` sink, but it suggests a different potential traversal issue than the one IRIS reported."
}"
}

The reported sink is real and exploitable. In export code, OpenOLAT takes a stored material path and uses it directly in `new File(questionContainer, exportPath)` / `new File(containerDir, exportPath)`, then creates parent directories and copies the file, with no normalization or `startsWith` containment check. The source is also valid, but it is second-order rather than direct request input: the path comes from QTI item content (`img src`, `a href`, `object data`) that an attacker can plant by importing a crafted QTI package into the question pool. The import code does have a check, but it only ensures the referenced file stays inside the original package root, not inside the item directory, so relative paths containing `..` are still accepted and preserved verbatim. When the same item is later exported into a different destination layout, that preserved relative path is reinterpreted against a new base directory and can traverse outside the intended export container. QTI21ExportProcessor.java:147-154 then does `String exportPath = material.getExportUri(); File exportFile = new File(questionContainer, exportPath); if(!exportFile.getParentFile().exists()) exportFile.getParentFile().mkdirs(); FileUtils.bcopy(originalFile, exportFile, ...)`; the same pattern exists at lines 250-257 for test/package export. There is no canonicalization before write. Import-side validation is insufficient: QTI21ImportProcessor.java:221-226 resolves the material path against the assessment item directory, normalizes it, and only checks `normalizedMaterialPath.startsWith(parentPath)` where `parentPath` is the package root; then it copies using `PathUtils.copyFileToDir(materialFile, outputFile.getParentFile(), material)`. `PathUtils.copyFileToDir` (PathUtils.java:58-63) again uses `new File(targetDir, path)` with no containment check, so a path like `../shared.png` is accepted as long as it stays inside the original package root. The raw `../...` string remains in the XML and later causes traversal on export. Reachability is real: imports are exposed via the UI (`ImportController.java:121-125`) and REST (`QuestionPoolWebService.java:151-171`), and exports are triggered by normal workflows such as content editor import, video question import, and assessment test composer insertion.", "new_reason:": "" }" }

Explaination: User-controlled HTTP header flows into file path resolution and a File object is created/checked for existence. The source is valid tainted input, and the sink is a filesystem path use. The shown sanitization is not demonstrated to block absolute or '..' traversal safely, so this path can be vulnerable to path traversal.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "This is a true positive. The source is real attacker-controlled input: `WebDAVDispatcherImpl.doCopy()/doMove()` read the HTTP `Destination` header via `getDestinationPath()`. The path is reachable and security-relevant because the resolved destination is not only checked for existence, but is later passed into delete/create/write logic in the same COPY/MOVE workflow. The sanitizer is insufficient: `RequestUtil.normalize()` explicitly says it is not a security check, and its implementation only removes `\"/../\"` sequences; a trailing `\"/..\"` survives. That means a destination such as `/webdav/groupfolders/<mountedGroup>/..` is accepted. From there, `VFSResourceRoot.resolveFile()` strips the leading slash and calls `base.resolve(name)`. For local folders, `VFSManager.resolveFile()` constructs `new File(baseAbsPath, childName)`; when `childName` is `..`, Java resolves it to the real parent directory on disk if it exists. This is not just theoretical: `GroupfoldersWebDAVMergeSource` mounts each writable group as a `LocalFolderImpl` rooted at `/cts/folders/BusinessGroup/<groupId>`, so one trailing `..` escapes to `/cts/folders/BusinessGroup`. Security callbacks are inherited by walking parent containers (`findInheritingSecurityCallbackContainer()`), so the escaped parent directory inherits the writable callback of the mounted child folder. In real operation, COPY/MOVE use overwrite-by-default; if the destination exists, `copyResource()` calls `deleteResource(destinationPath, ...)` before copying. Therefore a crafted destination ending in `..` can trigger recursive deletion or unauthorized write operations outside the intended WebDAV subtree. This is a feasible CWE-22/path traversal issue, not an FP caused by shallow data flow or unreachable control flow.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "1. Insufficient normalization: `RequestUtil.normalize()` warns it is not a security check and only collapses `\"/../\"`; it does not remove a trailing `\"/..\"` (src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/webdav/servlets/RequestUtil.java, normalize()). 2. User-controlled destination is used in COPY/MOVE: `getDestinationPath()` reads `req.getHeader(\"Destination\")`, URL-decodes it, normalizes it, and returns it to `copyResource(req, resp, moved)` (src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/webdav/servlets/WebDAVDispatcherImpl.java). 3. Escaping to parent on disk: in `VFSManager.resolveFile()`, for `LocalFolderImpl` the code does `String childName = extractChild(path); File t = new File(l.getBasefile().getAbsolutePath(), childName); if (t.exists()) ...`; with `childName == \"..\"`, this resolves to the real parent directory (src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/VFSManager.java). 4. Writable callback is inherited after escape: `findInheritingSecurityCallbackContainer()` walks parent containers until it finds a callback, so the escaped parent directory inherits the mounted folder's callback (src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/VFSManager.java). 5. Real writable mount: group folders are backed by local filesystem paths `\"/cts/folders/\" + type + \"/\" + id` and wrapped as writable `LocalFolderImpl` containers (src/main/java/org/olat/collaboration/CollaborationManagerImpl.java and src/main/java/org/olat/group/GroupfoldersWebDAVMergeSource.java). 6. Destructive use of destination: in COPY/MOVE, overwrite defaults to true, and if destination exists the code executes `deleteResource(destinationPath, req, resp, true, false)` before copying (src/main/java/org/olat/core/commons/services/webdav/servlets/WebDAVDispatcherImpl.java). A destination like `/webdav/groupfolders/<group>/..` therefore resolves to `/cts/folders/BusinessGroup` and can recursively delete that parent tree or be used as the base for subsequent writes.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "B1/source-sink check: the source is real attacker-controlled input. `PageCourseNode.importNode()` opens an imported `page.zip` from the course import directory (`PageCourseNode.java:228-235`). `PageImportExportHelper.importPage()` then reads `page.xml` from that ZIP and deserializes it with XStream (`PageImportExportHelper.java:191-195`, `PageXStream.java:105-127`). `MediaVersionImpl.storagePath` is deserialized and not sanitized, so the attacker controls `mediaZipPath`. The sink is also real: `PageServiceImpl.importMediaVersion()` iterates ZIP entries and, when `entryPath.startsWith(mediaZipPath)`, calls `unzip(...)` (`PageServiceImpl.java:406-418`). `unzip()` computes `fileName = entryPath.replace(mediaZipPath, \"\")`, builds `new File(mediaDir, fileName)`, and writes it via `FileUtils.copyToFile(...)` with no canonicalization or \"must stay under mediaDir\" check (`PageServiceImpl.java:449-457`, `FileUtils.java:464-468`).\nA1/A2 reachability check: this is not a shallow or unreachable path. It is the normal course/page import flow. An attacker can craft both `page.xml` and ZIP entry names in the imported archive. For example, if `page.xml` sets `storagePath` to `media/` and the ZIP contains an entry named `media/../../../../tmp/pwn.txt`, then `fileName` becomes `../../../../tmp/pwn.txt` and the write escapes `mediaDir`. The check `mediaFile.isHidden() || mediaFile.getName().startsWith(\".\")` does not stop this, because the basename `pwn.txt` is not hidden. No sanitizer normalizes the path or checks that the resolved target stays inside `mediaDir`.\nD-category/C1 check: there is no missed sanitizer in this path, and the report matches CWE-22/Zip Slip semantics. The vulnerability is exploitable by importing a malicious course/page package. Impact is arbitrary file write/overwrite within the filesystem permissions of the OpenOLAT process. The feature may require an authenticated user with import capability, but that does not make the report a false positive.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Concrete dataflow:\n1. `PageCourseNode.importNode()` loads `page.zip` from imported course content: `try(ZipFile zfile = new ZipFile(importPage)) { Page page = importHelper.importPage(zfile, null, owner); }` (`PageCourseNode.java:228-235`).\n2. `PageImportExportHelper.importPage()` reads attacker-controlled `page.xml` from the ZIP and passes the deserialized object into `pageService.importPage(...)` (`PageImportExportHelper.java:191-195`).\n3. `PageXStream` explicitly allows `MediaVersionImpl` and does not omit `storagePath`, so that field is controlled by `page.xml` (`PageXStream.java:75-107,125-127`; `MediaVersionImpl.java:174-180`).\n4. `PageServiceImpl.importMediaVersion()` uses that field as `mediaZipPath`: `String mediaZipPath = mediaVersion.getStoragePath(); ... if(entryPath.startsWith(mediaZipPath)) { File mediaFile = unzip(mediaZipPath, entry, storage, mediaDir); }` (`PageServiceImpl.java:406-418`).\n5. `unzip()` performs unsafe path construction: `String fileName = entryPath.replace(mediaZipPath, \"\"); File mediaFile = new File(mediaDir, fileName); FileUtils.copyToFile(in, mediaFile, \"\");` (`PageServiceImpl.java:451-457`).\n6. `FileUtils.copyToFile()` simply does `targetFile.getParentFile().mkdirs();` and writes the file; it does not validate containment (`FileUtils.java:464-468`).\nExample payload: `storagePath = \"media/\"` in `page.xml`, ZIP entry name = `media/../../../../tmp/pwn.txt`. This satisfies `startsWith(mediaZipPath)` and produces a traversal target outside `mediaDir`.",

}"

Explaination: ZipEntry name is attacker-controlled archive content and is used to build a File path. The regex only strips a prefix up to background/ and does not block absolute paths or remaining ../ segments, so writing the zip entry can escape the temp directory (Zip Slip/path traversal).",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "B1: The source and sink are correctly identified. `ZipEntry.getName()` is attacker-controlled from the uploaded ZIP, and `new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + \"/\" + fileName)` followed by `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)` is a real file-write sink. The only transformation on the entry name is `replaceAll(\".+background/\", \"\")` / `replaceAll(\".+teaser/\", \"\")`, which strips a prefix but does not reject `..`, absolute paths, or remaining separators. `WebappHelper.getMimeType(fileName)` checks only the filename extension, not path safety. A1/A2: the reported path is concretely reachable, not a hypothetical static-analysis artifact. During step-1 validation, `validateFormLogic()` calls `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()` for any uploaded ZIP. A crafted entry such as `Taxonomylevels_export/media/MATH/background/../../evil.png` passes the earlier structure checks, yields `finalZipEntry == \"/MATH/\"` so it matches a real taxonomy level, and later yields `fileName == \"../../evil.png\"` at the sink. D1/D3: there is no canonicalization, normalization, or containment check against the temp directory, so the write can escape `java.io.tmpdir`. The size check is also ineffective for a new target path because it checks `image.length()` before copying, which is `0` for a non-existent file. The feature is privilege-gated by `canImportExport()`, so this is an authenticated admin/taxonomy-import issue rather than an anonymous upload issue, but it is still a real CWE-22 arbitrary file write/overwrite within locations writable by the server process. The file write occurs during validation before the wizard is finished, so canceling later does not prevent the filesystem side effect.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reachable control/data flow: `validateFormLogic()` invokes `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()` when `uploadFileEl.getUploadFile() != null` ([TaxonomyImportStep1.java:389-408]). In `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()`, the ZIP entry is accepted if `zipEntry.getName().matches(uploadFileName + \"media/.+\")` and a taxonomy level exists for `finalZipEntry` derived by stripping `background/.*` or `teaser/.*` ([TaxonomyImportStep1.java:216-225, 246-250]). For entry `Taxonomylevels_export/media/MATH/background/../../evil.png`: `uploadFileName` becomes `Taxonomylevels_export/`, the regex still matches, `finalZipEntry` becomes `/MATH/`, and `matchedLevel` is found. Then `createTaxonomyLevelToImageMap()` computes `fileName = zipEntry.getName().replaceAll(\".+background/\", \"\")`, producing `../../evil.png`, checks only `IMAGE_MIME_TYPES.contains(WebappHelper.getMimeType(fileName))`, constructs `image = new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + \"/\" + fileName)`, and writes it via `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)` ([TaxonomyImportStep1.java:335-352]). `WebappHelper.getTmpDir()` returns `System.getProperty(\"java.io.tmpdir\")` ([WebappHelper.java:374-376]), so `../../evil.png` escapes the temp directory. There is no `normalize`, `getCanonicalPath`, or prefix check on the destination path. The expected archive layout is real: the exporter writes entries as `label + \"/media\" + taxonomyPath + \"background/\" + filename` / `...\"teaser/\" + filename` ([ExportTaxonomyLevels.java:206-216]), so the crafted path shape fits the actual import/export format. Exposure is privilege-gated: the import/export UI is shown only when `secCallback.canImportExport()` is true ([TaxonomyTreeTableController.java:179-183]), and `FullTaxonomySecurityCallback` returns `true` ([FullTaxonomySecurityCallback.java:44-46]).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

The random UUID key is only a temporary bearer token, not a sanitizer. In real deployments with the PDF module enabled, OpenOLAT hands `/pdfd/{key}/{rootFilename}` to an external renderer (AthenaPDF/Gotenberg). That renderer, or attacker-controlled HTML/JS rendered through the certificate-template feature, can observe the live key and issue a direct traversal request before `cache.remove(key)` runs.   "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "B1: the source and sink are correctly identified. `ValidationXmlSignatureController.doValidate()` accepts an uploaded XML signature file, `XMLDigitalSignatureUtil.getKeyName()` extracts the `<KeyName>` text verbatim, and `QTI21ServiceImpl.validateAssessmentResult()` uses that value in `new File(certificateFile.getParentFile(), keyName)` without canonicalization, path normalization, separator filtering, or a whitelist of allowed certificate names. This is not an A1/A2-only artifact: the path is directly reachable from a real upload workflow and can execute at runtime. D1 does not apply because there is no sanitizer on `keyName` before the file operation. The impact is somewhat limited because the feature is in a staff/admin validation workflow, but it is still a real CWE-22 issue: a crafted receipt can force the server to probe and open attacker-chosen local filesystem paths under the OpenOLAT process account.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reachable entry points exist in both `org/olat/ims/qti21/ui/assessment/ValidationXmlSignatureController.java` (`doValidate()` calls `qtiService.validateAssessmentResult(xmlSignature)`) and the UI buttons wired from `QTI21AdminController` / `IQIdentityListCourseNodeController`. The reported data flow is real: `XMLDigitalSignatureUtil.getKeyName()` returns the `<KeyName>` text unchanged; `QTI21ServiceImpl.validateAssessmentResult()` then does `File olderCertificateFile = new File(certificateFile.getParentFile(), keyName); if(olderCertificateFile.exists()) { kp = CryptoUtil.getX509CertificatePrivateKeyPairPfx(olderCertificateFile, ...); }`. The sink is stronger than an existence check: `CryptoUtil.getX509CertificatePrivateKeyPairPfx()` calls `keyStore.load(new FileInputStream(certificate), password.toCharArray())`, so the server really opens the chosen path. Exploitability is practical because `signDetached()` stores both `KeyName` and `X509Data` in `KeyInfo`, while fallback validation (`XMLDigitalSignatureUtil.validate(uri, assessmentResult, xmlSignature, false)`) uses `X509KeySelector` to read the embedded `X509Data`. Therefore an attacker can take a legitimately signed receipt, modify only `<KeyName>` to something like `../../../etc/passwd` or an absolute path, and keep the original signature otherwise valid. If the target path does not exist, the code falls back to embedded-certificate validation and the receipt can still validate; if the path exists but is not a valid/readable keystore for the configured password, the method throws and returns `notValid`. That difference gives a real local file existence/readability oracle, and the arbitrary path open itself is sufficient to qualify as path traversal.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported path is a false positive. IRIS correctly sees that `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` returns the remote SharePoint/OneDrive item name (`driveItem.name()`), and that `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.toFile(VFSItem)` would convert an arbitrary `getRelPath()` into `VFSManager.olatRootFile(relPath)`. However, the report misses the real runtime semantics of this type. `DriveItemLeaf` is a `VFSExternalLeaf`/`VFSExternalItem`, and `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.getMetadataFor(VFSItem)` has a dedicated branch for external items that returns `externalItem.getMetaInfo()` directly instead of calling `toFile()` on them. In the normal design, SharePoint-backed items are handled as external objects, not as local files under `bcroot`. In addition, nearly all versioning/edit paths that call into `addVersion()` are gated by `canVersion() == YES`; `DriveItemLeaf.canVersion()` is hardcoded `NO`, so those paths do not normally accept SharePoint leaves. The tool therefore stitched together a cross-type path from an external VFS item into a local-file sink without respecting the external-item control flow and type split.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D4.Type/Structure Blindness"
  ],
  "evidence": "Source: `DriveItemLeaf.getRelPath()` returns `driveItem.name()` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:87-88`). But `DriveItemLeaf` is an external type and is non-versionable/read-only: `canWrite() == NO` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:146-147`), `canVersion() == NO` (`DriveItemLeaf.java:156-157`). The repository service explicitly special-cases external items: `if(path instanceof VFSExternalItem externalItem) { return externalItem.getMetaInfo(); }` before any `toFile()` call (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:345-351`). This defeats the report's main external-to-local sink path for normal metadata handling. The claimed sink conversion exists (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.toFile(VFSItem)` -> `VFSManager.olatRootFile(relPath)` at `VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:504-506`, `VFSManager.java:90-92`), but it is not the standard path used for `DriveItemLeaf`. Real callers also usually require versionable/local leaves before calling `addVersion()`, e.g. WebDAV only versions when `childLeaf.canVersion() == YES` (`VFSResourceRoot.java:180-182`), and file-copy overwrite uses the target existing local item, not the external source, for `addVersion()` (`FileCopyController.java:230-231`). There is one suspicious separate bug in `CmdMoveCopy` that calls `addVersion(sourceLeaf, ...)` on the source when the target is versioned, but that is not the path reported by IRIS and does not change that the reported path itself ignores the `VFSExternalItem` type split.",

  "analysis": "This is a true positive. The source is correctly identified: `zipEntry.getName()` is attacker-controlled ZIP metadata from the uploaded archive. The effective sink is the filesystem write at `TaxonomyImportStep1.java:352`, where the `File` built from `WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + \"/\" + fileName` is passed to `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)`. The path is concretely reachable at runtime: `importTaxonomyMediaFiles()` iterates over all uploaded ZIP entries, accepts entries matching `<prefix>media/.+`, derives `finalZipEntry` by stripping `background/.*` or `teaser/.*`, and calls `createTaxonomyLevelToImageMap()` when the corresponding taxonomy level exists or is being imported. A crafted entry like `demo/media/MATH/background/../../../../escape.png` makes `finalZipEntry` become `/MATH/` so `matchedLevel` can be valid, while `fileName` becomes `../../../../escape.png`. There is no sanitizer: the regex only strips the prefix up to `background/` or `teaser/`; it does not reject `..` segments, and there is no canonicalization or `normalize()+startsWith(tmpDir)` check. `WebappHelper.getMimeType(fileName)` only checks the extension, not the path, and the size check uses `image.length()` before the file is written, so a new target file appears as size 0 and passes. Pure absolute-path injection is not the main issue here, but `../` traversal is sufficient to escape the temp directory. This is not an A1/A2 unreachable-path case and not a D1 missed-sanitizer false positive; the write primitive is real.",

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source and sink are reachable, but the reported CWE-022 is not actually exploitable. `request.getPathInfo()` is attacker-controlled and eventually influences filesystem lookups, so the source/sink selection is broadly reasonable (not B1). The reported path is also real and reachable through `/raw/*`, so this is not an A1/A2 control-flow issue. The false positive comes from missing the sanitizer and the actual Java path semantics (D1): before any file access, `StaticServlet` always passes the user-controlled segment through `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` (`StaticServlet.java:92,102,126,173`). That routine normalizes separators, resolves `/./` and `/../`, and returns `null` when traversal would escape above the context root (`ServletUtil.java:685-691`). Any `null` result is immediately rejected with `sendNotFound()` (`StaticServlet.java:93-95,103-105,127-129`). Paths ending in `..` can at most resolve to a directory and are then blocked by `staticFile.isDirectory()` with `sendForbidden()` (`StaticServlet.java:190-196`). For surviving paths, the lookup remains confined under the static base: `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)` does not escape merely because `normalizedRelPath` starts with `/`; on the local JDK 21 runtime, `new File(\"/tmp/base\", \"/a/b\").getCanonicalPath()` resolves to `/tmp/base/a/b`. The fallback `getRealPath()` calls also prepend `/static`, so after normalization they resolve context-relative static paths, not arbitrary host files. Therefore the report is a false positive caused by overlooked sanitization/runtime path handling, not a real path traversal.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source and sink are real: `HttpServletRequest.getPathInfo()` is user-controlled, and the resulting `File` is later used by `file.lastModified()` and, if accepted, by `FileInputStream`. So this is not a B1 source/sink-selection mistake. The reported control-flow is also reachable through the `/raw/*` servlet mapping, so A1/A2 are not the main issue. The report is a false positive because the code includes a real sanitizer that the analysis effectively missed: `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` canonicalizes the path, resolves `/../`, and returns `null` when the request would escape above the intended root; `StaticServlet` immediately turns that into `404`. As a result, attacker inputs such as `../etc/passwd` or `a/../../b` do not reach any file access outside the static roots. Surviving paths remain within either `userdata/customizing/static/` or the webapp `/static` tree, and directory-only edge cases such as `/..` are rejected with `isDirectory()` -> forbidden. In real-world use, this endpoint serves static resources; it does not let an external attacker traverse to arbitrary filesystem paths. The primary FP cause is D1 (missed sanitizer / missed key program point).",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],
  "evidence": "`StaticServlet.doGet()` uses `String normalizedRelPath = ServletUtil.normalizePath(staticRelPath);` and immediately checks `if (normalizedRelPath == null) { DispatcherModule.sendNotFound(response); }` before creating any `File`. In the reported branch it then does `File file = new File(folderManager.getRootFile(), normalizedRelPath);` and rejects directories with `if(file.isDirectory()) { DispatcherModule.sendForbidden(response); }`. `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` explicitly normalizes separators, forces a leading `/`, collapses `//` and `/./`, and most importantly resolves `/../` with `if (index == 0) return null;` when traversal would move above the root. Concrete example: request `/raw/static/../../etc/passwd` gives `staticRelPath = \"../etc/passwd\"`; after normalization the string starts with `/../`, so `normalizePath()` returns `null` and the servlet returns 404. Also, a surviving leading `/` does not create an absolute-path escape here: on the local Unix JDK, `java.io.UnixFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` concatenates `parent + child` when `child` starts with `/`; and in OpenJDK Windows `WinNTFileSystem.resolve`, a single leading slash is dropped (`childStart = 1`) and the remainder is appended to the parent, not treated as a breakout path. Official Windows source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openjdk/jdk/master/src/java.base/windows/classes/java/io/WinNTFileSystem.java",

In the info-message feature, `attachmentPath` is generated from a local VFS container under the OpenOLAT storage root, and the tool incorrectly stitched that flow to the unrelated SharePoint `getName()` implementation via the polymorphic `VFSItem.getName()` call inside `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath()`. Concretely, the runtime object passed to `getRelativeItemPath()` here is a local `LocalFolderImpl` for the attachment folder, not a `DriveItemLeaf`. Therefore the report relies on incorrect type/structure reasoning and does not show a real CWE-22 path traversal reachable through normal application use.",

  "evidence": "1) `InfoDisplayController` stores the field only from `infoMessageManager.storeAttachment(...)` (`src/main/java/org/olat/commons/info/ui/InfoDisplayController.java:495-499`). 2) In `storeAttachment(...)`, `folderName` is either `datePart + \"_attachments\"` or the last segment of the existing path (`split(\"/\")` then `parts[parts.length - 1]`), then `attachmentFolder = VFSManager.getOrCreateContainer(ressourceContainer, folderName)`, and the returned/stored value is `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath(attachmentFolder, getStoragePath(), null)` (`src/main/java/org/olat/commons/info/manager/InfoMessageFrontendManagerImpl.java:171-188`). So `attachmentPath` is an internally generated folder path under the info-message storage tree, not an uploaded file name and not a SharePoint item name. 3) The report hinges on `VFSManager.getRelativeItemPath()` calling `tmpItem.getName()` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/VFSManager.java:545-558`), but at this call site `tmpItem` is the local attachment folder and its local parent containers. The tool conflated interface-level `getName()` implementations and treated it as if it were `DriveItemLeaf.getName()` from the SharePoint module. 4) Even attacker-controlled upload names do not escape the attachment folder: `LocalFolderImpl.createChildLeaf()` checks `isInPath(name)` using a normalized path and rejects names outside the parent (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/LocalFolderImpl.java:480-485,530-549`), and it strips a leading slash in `cleanFilename()` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/vfs/LocalFolderImpl.java:559-563`). Upload names are also normalized earlier by `FileUtils.cleanFilename(...)` (`src/main/java/org/olat/core/util/FileUtils.java:919-950`). 5) The sink `new File(root, infoMessage.getAttachmentPath())` (`src/main/java/org/olat/commons/info/manager/InfoMessageFrontendManagerImpl.java:267-275`) therefore consumes an internal storage path, not attacker-controlled traversal input.   "analysis": "The reported source (`HttpServletRequest.getPathInfo()`) and sink (`FileInputStream` on a `File`) are real, but this is not a real CWE-22 in the shown path. This is not a Type A2 unreachable-path issue; the servlet path is reachable. The false positive comes from missing the concrete effect of the sanitizer and Java path-resolution semantics. `StaticServlet` normalizes every request-derived path before file access, and returns 404 if normalization fails. `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` replaces backslashes, forces a leading slash, collapses `//` and `/./`, and rejects traversal above the context by returning `null` when `/../` would escape. Any remaining edge cases such as a trailing `/..` or `/.` resolve only to a directory, and the servlet returns 403 for directories before any file read.
