  "evidence": "Code evidence: `StaticServlet.doGet()` normalizes all request-derived variants before use (`StaticServlet.java:91-103`, `125-131`, `172-174`). The sanitizer in `ServletUtil.normalizePath()` (`ServletUtil.java:653-695`) does the following: it converts backslashes to `/`, prepends `/` if missing, removes `//` and `/./`, and for `/../` it returns `null` when the traversal would move above the root; otherwise it removes the previous segment. `StaticServlet` immediately sends 404 when normalization returns `null`. After that, `deliverStatic()` builds `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)` (`StaticServlet.java:156`) and blocks directories with `staticFile.isDirectory()` (`StaticServlet.java:192-196`). Therefore `/raw/<ver>/../../WEB-INF/web.xml` is rejected during normalization, while `/raw/<ver>/..` only resolves to the parent directory of `static` and is denied as a directory, not read as a file. The fallback branch is also normalized first (`StaticServlet.java:172-174`) and still forced under `/static` via `getContextRealPath(STATIC_DIR_NAME + fallbackPath)`. Supporting Java semantics: OpenJDK `UnixFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` appends slash-leading children under `parent`, and OpenJDK `WinNTFileSystem.resolve(parent, child)` drops the leading slash and appends the rest under `parent`; it does not turn `/foo` into a filesystem-root escape. Reference: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openjdk/jdk8u/master/jdk/src/windows/classes/java/io/WinNTFileSystem.java",

The sink is only exercised for deleted local folders with persisted repository metadata; the report incorrectly stitched together a SharePoint VFS path and a local-trash maintenance path.",
  "FP_Reason": ["A2"],
  "evidence": "`DriveItemContainer` is not handled like a local repository file: `canMeta()` returns `VFSStatus.NO` and `getMetaInfo()` returns `null` (`DriveItemContainer.java:97-109`), while `getRelPath()` only returns `driveItem.name()` (`DriveItemContainer.java:113-115`). SharePoint containers also do not support delete/trash operations: `AbstractVirtualContainer.delete()` and `deleteSilently()` both return `VFSSuccess.ERROR_FAILED` (`AbstractVirtualContainer.java:126-139`). In contrast, the reported sink is only reached from local filesystem deletion code: `LocalFolderImpl.delete()` moves a local folder into `._ootrash`, gets repository metadata for that local folder, and then calls `vfsRepositoryService.cleanTrash(doer, vfsMetadata)` (`LocalFolderImpl.java:314-355`). `cleanTrash()` itself immediately requires `trashMetadata != null && trashMetadata.isDeleted()` and then walks persisted descendants from the metadata DAO (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:912-924`). For external items, `getMetadataFor(VFSItem)` does not materialize a local file path; it simply returns `externalItem.getMetaInfo()` (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:345-348`), which is `null` for `DriveItemContainer`. Also, `getItemFor(VFSMetadata)` only reconstructs transient metadata through `inMemoryItems` (`VFSRepositoryServiceImpl.java:429-437`), while SharePoint metadata is transient (`DriveItemMetadata.java:32`) and not the deleted local `VFSMetadataImpl` objects consumed by `cleanTrash()`. So the control-flow path assumed by the report is unreachable.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"

Explaination: User-controlled request path reaches File construction and then file read/length on that File. The shown normalizePath does not demonstrate protection against absolute paths or '..' traversal, so this can expose arbitrary files.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source is real: `request.getPathInfo()` is attacker-controlled and `StaticServlet` is publicly mapped to `/raw/*`. But this does not become CWE-022 in practice. Every path from `pathInfo` to `new File(...)` first goes through `ServletUtil.normalizePath(...)`, and `doGet()` aborts with `sendNotFound()` when that function returns `null`. `normalizePath()` canonicalizes separators, removes `/./`, resolves `/../`, and rejects attempts to escape above the root by returning `null` when `/../` appears at the beginning after reduction. So exploit payloads such as `../...`, `../../etc/passwd`, or backslash variants do not reach the file read sink. For inputs that do reach the sink, the path remains confined to the configured static directories. The report also overstates the absolute-path risk: the local JDK `File(parent, child)` implementation resolves a leading-`/` child against the parent rather than discarding the parent, so `new File(staticAbsPath, normalizedRelPath)` does not turn `/etc/passwd` into a read of the system file. Therefore this report is a false positive.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "1) `StaticServlet` guards all reported flows with normalization: at `StaticServlet.java` lines 91-96, 101-108, and 125-131, the servlet computes `normalizedRelPath = ServletUtil.normalizePath(staticRelPath)` and immediately returns 404 when it is `null`; only non-null values reach `new File(...)`. The fallback path is also normalized at lines 172-174. 2) The sanitizer is explicit in `ServletUtil.java` lines 667-695: it replaces `\\\\` with `/`, prepends `/` if missing, collapses `//`, removes `/./`, and for `/../` executes `if (index == 0) return null;` before removing the parent segment. Thus `../` traversal attempts are blocked before file IO. 3) The JDK semantics do not create an absolute-path escape here: local JDK source `java.io.File(String parent, String child)` calls `FS.resolve(FS.normalize(parent), FS.normalize(child))`, and local `UnixFileSystem.resolve` does `if (child.charAt(0) == '/') { if (parent.equals('/')) return child; return trimSeparator(parent + child); }`. So a child like `/etc/passwd` becomes `<staticAbsPath>/etc/passwd`, not the system `/etc/passwd`. 4) Real runtime effect: malicious traversal strings take the `normalizedRelPath == null` branch and never reach `FileInputStream`; benign strings only address files under `/static` or the custom static folder.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

Explaination: ZipEntry name is end-user controlled from an uploaded ZIP. The code strips a prefix but does not block absolute paths or '..' segments before creating/writing the File, so this can enable Zip Slip/path traversal.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "TP. The report identifies a real attacker-controlled source and a real file-write sink. `ZipEntry.getName()` comes from an uploaded ZIP and is used to derive `fileName` in `createTaxonomyLevelToImageMap()`. That `fileName` is concatenated into `new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + \"/\" + fileName)` and then written via `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)`. There is no canonical-path validation, no `..` rejection, and no normalization check that the resolved path stays under the temp directory. The MIME check is not a sanitizer; it only checks the extension through `WebappHelper.getMimeType(fileName)`, so traversal names ending in `.png/.jpg/.gif` can still pass. The path is concretely reachable because the taxonomy matching logic strips everything after `teaser/` or `background/`, so a malicious filename can be appended after a valid taxonomy path. One nuance: the report's mention of absolute paths is imprecise because the code prefixes `java.io.tmpdir`; however `../` traversal is sufficient to escape the intended directory, so the finding remains a true CWE-022 issue.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant flow: `TaxonomyImportStep1.java:216-225` accepts ZIP entries under `.../media/...` and computes `finalZipEntry` by removing `background/.*` or `teaser/.*`; `TaxonomyImportStep1.java:338-352` then computes `fileName = zipEntry.getName().replaceAll(\".+teaser/\", \"\")` (or background equivalent), builds `image = new File(WebappHelper.getTmpDir() + \"/\" + fileName)`, and writes with `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile(in, image)`.\n\nConcrete exploit shape: exported legitimate entries are created as `label + MEDIA_FOLDER + taxonomyPath + \"teaser/\" + imageName` or `... + \"background/\" + imageName` (`ExportTaxonomyLevels.java:210-218`). An attacker can upload a ZIP entry like `label/media/<validTaxonomyPath>teaser/../../../../outside.png`. Then:\n- the taxonomy-path check still succeeds because `finalZipEntry` removes `teaser/.*` and sees only `<validTaxonomyPath>` (`TaxonomyImportStep1.java:222-225`);\n- `fileName` becomes `../../../../outside.png` (`TaxonomyImportStep1.java:338-339`);\n- the destination becomes `<java.io.tmpdir>/../../../../outside.png`, where `java.io.tmpdir` comes from `WebappHelper.getTmpDir()` (`WebappHelper.java:374-375`);\n- `FileUtils.copyInputStreamToFile` writes attacker-controlled bytes to that resolved filesystem path.\n\nThis is reachable in the real UI: `TaxonomyTreeTableController.java:179-180` exposes import/export when `secCallback.canImportExport()`, `TaxonomyTreeTableController.java:502-511` opens the import wizard, and real admin callers use `TaxonomySecurityCallback.FULL`, whose implementation returns `true` for import/export (`FullTaxonomySecurityCallback.java:44`). The write happens already during step-1 validation (`TaxonomyImportStep1.java:389-408`), before the wizard is finished. No sanitizer in this path blocks `..` traversal.",

}"
